question: Explain the primary difference between a process and a thread. Why might an application use multiple threads within a single process instead of multiple processes?
expectedPoints: A process is an independent program in execution with its own memory space, while a thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process that shares the process's memory and resources.
Threads are more efficient to create and manage (context switching between threads is faster) because they operate within the same address space.
Communication between threads is simpler and faster (shared memory) compared to inter-process communication (IPC) which requires mechanisms like pipes or shared memory segments.

question: Define thrashing in the context of memory management. What is its primary cause and how can an operating system work to prevent or resolve it?
expectedPoints: Thrashing occurs when a system spends more time swapping pages between memory and disk (paging) than executing useful work, severely degrading performance.
The primary cause is insufficient physical memory (RAM) to hold the working sets of the active processes, leading to a high page fault rate.

The operating system can resolve thrashing by employing a local page replacement algorithm (like Working Set or Page Fault Frequency) to temporarily suspend (swap out) one or more processes, reducing the degree of multiprogramming and allowing the remaining processes to have adequate frames.

question: List two primary responsibilities of the operating system in file management.
expectedPoints: To provide a logical, user-friendly abstraction for storing information (the file) and organizing files (the directory structure).
To manage the physical storage of files on secondary storage devices (like hard disks), including mapping logical file blocks to physical disk sectors and managing free space.
